---
title: "Workplace Injury Risk"
subtitle: "Office Workers vs. Manufacturing Workers"
author: "Brynn Baumert"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    self-contained: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(readxl)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

r8_data <- read_excel(
  "case-and-demographic-characteristics-table-r8-2023-2024.xlsx",
  col_names = FALSE)

r8_data <- r8_data[-c(1,2), ]

names(r8_data)[2]  <- "naics"
names(r8_data)[3]  <- "case_type"
names(r8_data)[4]  <- "total_rate"
names(r8_data)[10] <- "fall_rate"

mfg <- r8_data %>% filter(naics == "31-33", case_type == "DAFW")
fin <- r8_data %>% filter(naics == "52",    case_type == "DAFW")
pro <- r8_data %>% filter(naics == "54",    case_type == "DAFW")

mfg_total <- as.numeric(mfg$total_rate[1])
mfg_falls <- as.numeric(mfg$fall_rate[1])
fin_total  <- as.numeric(fin$total_rate[1])
fin_falls  <- as.numeric(fin$fall_rate[1])
pro_total  <- as.numeric(pro$total_rate[1])
pro_falls  <- as.numeric(pro$fall_rate[1])

mfg_fall_share <- round(mfg_falls / mfg_total * 100, 1)
fin_fall_share  <- round(fin_falls  / fin_total  * 100, 1)
pro_fall_share  <- round(pro_falls  / pro_total  * 100, 1)

fatal_df <- as.data.frame(read_excel("fatal.xlsx", col_names = FALSE))

mfg_fatal_total <- as.numeric(fatal_df[143, 3])
mfg_fatal_falls <- as.numeric(fatal_df[143, 7])
fin_fatal_total <- as.numeric(fatal_df[453, 3])
fin_fatal_falls <- as.numeric(fatal_df[453, 7])
pro_fatal_total <- as.numeric(fatal_df[488, 3])
pro_fatal_falls <- as.numeric(fatal_df[488, 7])

mfg_fall_pct <- round(mfg_fatal_falls / mfg_fatal_total * 100, 1)
fin_fall_pct  <- round(fin_fatal_falls  / fin_fatal_total  * 100, 1)
pro_fall_pct  <- round(pro_fatal_falls  / pro_fatal_total  * 100, 1)
```


## Which Worker Do You Think Is at Greater Risk?

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](manu.jpg)

**Manufacturing Worker**
Sparks. Machinery. Hard hats.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](fall.jpg)

**Office Worker**
Spreadsheets. Coffee. Stairs.
:::

::::

## Research Question

A workplace safety officer needs to decide where to focus resources.

> Are office workers at greater risk of injury from falls than manufacturing
> workers — and how does injury severity compare?

Most people assume falls are a manufacturing problem, but the data paints a slightly different picture

## Why This Matters

- Workplace injuries cost U.S. employers billions each year in lost
  productivity, workers' compensation, and healthcare
- Safety officers have limited budgets — they need to know where to focus
- If falls are primarily a manufacturing problem, office programs can focus
  elsewhere
- But if falls dominate the office injury profile, that changes how employers approach injury prevention

## Data Sources

Two federal datasets from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics:

| Dataset | What it measures | Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| **SOII Table R8** | Nonfatal injury rates by industry | 2023–2024 |
| **CFOI Table A-1** | Fatal injury counts by industry | 2024 |

Both are free, public domain, and published annually.

## What is DAFW?

**DAFW = Days Away From Work**

- The BLS uses DAFW to measure the most serious nonfatal injuries
- An injury counts as DAFW only if the worker had to stay home completely —
  not just work light duty
- Rates are reported per **10,000 full-time equivalent workers**
- We focus on DAFW because it is the most consistent and comparable measure
  across all industries

## Overall Injury Rates

Manufacturing workers get injured much more often overall.

```{r echo=FALSE}
tibble(
  Sector = c("Manufacturing", "Finance & Insurance", "Professional Services"),
  `Total DAFW Rate (per 10,000)` = c(mfg_total, fin_total, pro_total)
) %>% kable()
```

Manufacturing is **`r round(mfg_total / fin_total, 0)`x** higher than finance
and insurance. This is what most people would expect — factory work is
physically demanding and carries obvious hazards.

## But Falls Are a Different Story

When we look at falls *specifically*, the proportion shifts.

```{r echo=FALSE}
tibble(
  Sector = c("Manufacturing", "Finance & Insurance", "Professional Services"),
  `Fall Rate` = c(mfg_falls, fin_falls, pro_falls),
  `Falls as % of All Injuries` = paste0(
    c(mfg_fall_share, fin_fall_share, pro_fall_share), "%")
) %>% kable()
```

Manufacturing has more falls in absolute terms — but falls make up a
larger *share* of injuries in office environments.

## What That Means in Practice

- Out of every **100 injured manufacturing workers**, about
  **`r mfg_fall_share`** were hurt from a fall
- Out of every **100 injured finance workers**, about
  **`r fin_fall_share`** were hurt from a fall

In manufacturing, workers also face machinery, overexertion, and contact
incidents. In an office, **falls are the more dominant hazard** — there isn't much
else competing with them.

## Fatal Injuries: CFOI Data

Does the same pattern hold for workplace deaths?

```{r echo=FALSE}
tibble(
  Sector = c("Manufacturing", "Finance & Insurance", "Professional Services"),
  `Total Deaths` = c(mfg_fatal_total, fin_fatal_total, pro_fatal_total),
  `Deaths from Falls` = c(mfg_fatal_falls, fin_fatal_falls, pro_fatal_falls),
  `Falls as % of Deaths` = paste0(c(mfg_fall_pct, fin_fall_pct, pro_fall_pct), "%")
) %>% kable()
```

Yes — falls caused **`r fin_fall_pct`%** of deaths in finance and insurance,
versus **`r mfg_fall_pct`%** in manufacturing.

## Key Takeaway

- Manufacturing has far higher injury rates **overall**
- But falls make up a larger **share** of injuries in office settings —
  in both nonfatal and fatal data
- This is consistent across two independent datasets with different
  methodologies
- That consistency strengthens confidence in the finding


## Recommendation

Office safety programs should prioritize fall prevention because:

- Falls are the **single most common** injury type in office environments
- The pattern holds at every level of severity — minor injuries and deaths
- Prevention is relatively low cost:
  - Slip-resistant flooring and mats
  - Clear walkway policies
  - Basic employee awareness training

## Limitations

limits:

- **Self-reporting:** SOII relies on employer OSHA 300 Logs — some injuries
  may go unreported
- **Broad categories:** NAICS 52 and 54 include workers beyond pure desk
  roles — some do fieldwork or client visits
- **Missing severity measure:** Median days away from work would strengthen
  the severity comparison but was not available in these tables
- **Different time periods:** SOII covers 2023–2024, CFOI covers 2024 only

## Conclusion

The original question: *are office workers at greater fall risk than
manufacturing workers?*

**The answer:**

- Not in absolute terms — manufacturing is more dangerous overall
- But falls are the **dominant hazard** in office environments in a way
  they are not in manufacturing (holds for nonfatal and fatal data)

## Thats all, stay safe on the job

**Data sources:**

- BLS Survey of Occupational Injuries and Illnesses (SOII), 2023–2024
- BLS Census of Fatal Occupational Injuries (CFOI), 2024
